protocol: medical - supraventricular tachycardia (including atrial fibrillation) medical - tachycardia medical - ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (protocol 2 - 8)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having chest pain and shortness of breath for about an hour now.
Can you tell me the current year, month, and day?
2020, July, the 25th.
Mr. Smith, I'm going to check your airway, breathing, and pulse now.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is your chest pain right now?
When did the chest pain start exactly?
Does anything make it better or worse, like movement or breathing?
Can you describe the type of pain-like sharp, dull, or aching?
Does the pain go anywhere else, such as your arm or jaw?
Are you dizzy, coughing, or having any nausea?
And what time do you think it is right now?
Around 4:10 p.m.
Okay, so you're alert and oriented to time, place, person, and situation-A&Ox4. That's good.
Let's continue with your airway assessment. Your airway appears patent. Do you have any difficulty breathing or unusual sounds in your throat?
Your breathing looks adequate. I'll auscultate your lungs. Is that okay with you?
Your breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Now, checking your circulation. Your radial pulse is irregular.
Pulse is 170 and irregular with occasional extra beats, known as ectopy.
Respiratory rate is 20, unlabored and regular.
Blood pressure is 117/75. Heart rate is high but blood pressure is stable.
Oxygen saturation is 98%. That's good.
Blood glucose is 82. It's within the normal range.
You're currently in Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response and frequent premature ventricular contractions.
You mentioned the chest pain started suddenly. You also feel lightheaded. How bad is the lightheadedness?
You are allergic to amiodarone and rocephin-understood. We are using metoprolol instead.
Your regular medications are metoprolol, cardizem, and xarelto.
You had an aortic valve replacement, CABG, previous MI, and a pacemaker implanted-correct?
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
What were you doing when the symptoms started?
About how long has the pain been going on now?
Your hands are trembling. You mentioned detoxing from alcohol and haven't had a drink since yesterday-is that right?
No pale skin or pallor observed.
Your pupils are equal and reactive. No signs of facial droop or slurred speech.
No discharge from ears or nose.
Listening to your breath sounds to check for any wheezing or additional sounds.
No irregular heart sounds heard just now.
Let me check your abdomen. There doesn't appear to be any discomfort or guarding.
No blood in your urine or urgency reported.
Your legs and arms seem to be showing normal use and strength.
Temperature is 98.2° in the ear. No fever reported.
SpO2 went to 99% briefly earlier.
We'll place you on a cardiac monitor. Is that okay with you?
A 4-lead ECG was just placed to monitor and stabilize you.
A 12-lead ECG was just obtained. It shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and multiple premature contractions.
We're about to give you one dose of 324mg chewable aspirin. That's okay with you?
A 20 Gauge IV has been started in the hand successfully.
You had episodes of supraventricular tachycardia along with the atrial fibrillation.
Your pulse is still at 170, blood pressure 117/75, and oxygen at 98%.
I see you have significant cardiac history-valve replacement, bypass surgery, previous MI, and current pacemaker-correct?
You've also been diagnosed with atrial fibrillation and supraventricular tachycardia-yes?
You mentioned lightheadedness with your chest pain. How is that now?
Do you have any numbness or weakness on one side? Or do you have any slurred speech or facial drooping?
Just reconfirming your history includes atrial fibrillation, a pacemaker, and previous heart surgery.
Thanks for informing us about your amiodarone and rocephin allergy-we're using metoprolol instead.
You also mentioned not drinking much water today. That can contribute to the irregular heartbeat and lightheaded feeling-is that correct?
You will receive 2.5mg of metoprolol via IV to help slow your heart rate.
Let's recheck your vitals now. Pulse is 119; BP is 101/73; oxygen is 100%.
Your blood glucose remains at 0, but you haven't eaten today. We will keep that in mind.
Earlier, it was 99% but has now stabilized at 100%.
The pain is still sharp and localized with no spreading.
No signs of ST-elevation MI, but we're closely monitoring.
You don't have any confusion or trouble with your balance or coordination.
We've followed the tachycardia and chest pain protocol including IV fluids and metoprolol, authorized by medical control.
Mr. Smith, you're now on the stretcher. You're secure for transport.
Transporting you directly to VCU cardiovascular services.
Your pulse is now 119; blood pressure 101/73; oxygen remains at 100%.
Metoprolol was successfully administered.
The pain is still sharp with no change in sensation.
Blood pressure is now 117/80.
SpO2 remains at 100%.
Metoprolol 2.5mg was successfully administered with improvement noted.
You're showing positive response to the treatment. Let's continue to monitor.
You're secure and ready for transport now.
Contacting the VCU ER staff for an update on our arrival.
Handoff to ER complete. We are now en route.
